author: @googacct
text: The downside I have found with Dremel style tools is that they are too powerful. If the bit grabs, there is a good chance that the tool will jerk and  cut someplace you do not want.  My preference is to use an air pencil grinder. They have a lower torque and will slow down rather than grab. I find that they work almost as fast as the Dremel style too. I have also found dental drills useful for working on small parts as well.

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text:
if you have that issue you are pushing it too hard, or using the wrong point, and need to get one with a finer grade of cutting blades, *or switch to something with a negative rake versus the positive rake of say a saw blade, 

I overcome this issue with those saw blades you can get that are super thin and tiny by switching the blade backwards so it is a negative rake it is cutting with, and it increases control drastically and eliminates almost entirely the "grab" you mention. This negative vs positive rake trick you can use on a LOT of things too,

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: I have to wonder if there are diamond impregnated tile saw blades that could be used in place of the dirty rock and rock with fibers cut off discs as I have used them on a circular saw and they really work a treat compared to the bigger cut off discs you can get for a saw to chop on road material and concrete ect. as well as cutting steel, the diamond impregnated one I have cut concrete 3 inches deep about half the speed my carbide rip saw blade cuts 2x4 material and then taken it to steel pipe and it barely touched it before the pipe was cut through and dropped off. So seems like those would be excellent for a dremel cut off blade.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i don't know mate - most of the ones i have used have been the fibre and grit type - but i am going to look out now!

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: oh my god, wow I am glad I have a 20 year old dremel here, since the brush covers are free to unscrew them so you can change out the brushes and such any time you wish. Them sealing that in so you can't do that is preposterous. (still wondering why the brushes have not been replaced with a roller bearing system where the outer cover of a roller bearing sleeve or race is pressed against the commuter to remove the bulk of the wear and tear that happens with a flat block of graphite being ground down till it is mated to the commuter ring.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it does eem a really poor decision on dremels part

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering yeah especially since the foredom hanging rotary tool is old enough that you can get knockoffs that are sufficient, and just grab an official foredom handle to slap on the snake and have all the precision and accuracy for a fraction of the price. (same with swapping out parts like official foredom snake parts or whole snake, and foot pedal, and still be at like 150 dollars for all that comes in the foredom kit for 250 dollars.

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering my whole point about the foredom is that all that tech is out from under patent period, and I am guessing most of the older dremel tech from that long ago is also, and all these new design changes might be over patenting renewal to control supply and such. So the cheaper knockoff products might be using the older superior designs that are serviceable and can even be used to get replacement parts for older dremels that dremel no longer supports with replacement repair parts and such. Since you can do just that with Foredom tools though unlike dremel Foredom did not go the redesign to patent over and over route, they just ride on their name and quality control being better than the others.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
the dremel and such are actually weaker smaller versions of the "foredom" rotary tool that hangs on a hook with a snake and handles you can change out, some what self centering and all, I have two of them, and tons of the T40 handles with lots of collets from 1/4 to 1/32 inch in diameter, and its going to be the design for my CNC rig so the bulk of the tool is not hanging off the Z axis, just a snake running down to a handle that is mounted on the head :) (also modified a shaft for a collet system that goes up to half inch diameter and a better self centering system XD

of course this is all going on my system I am putting my replicator level 3D printing upgrades on, to test them out. :)

author: @jeffreycoulter4095
text:
Two recommendations 
1. This is a tool, not a toy, don't cannibalize it.
2. Where is it made? That information is becoming more and more important. I would spend more money for products made in the USA, Canada, Europe and Australia. Why? Because it's my money and for the moment, we do have some say.

Your review was top notch as always

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @dav1dbone
text: "spare collet chuck", lol, he brings pulls out that rectangular block thing that nobody knows what it's for. Btw big rotary tool/Dremel fan for 20years+..... Endless uses, so handy and have invested a fair amount on these, settled now on two cordless, Dremels, my original 8000, and the 12volt 8220 - an absolutely fantastic tool. However the mains powered rotary tools are obviously more capable and don't require charging, everyone should have one in their "tool box", they're just so handy. Amazing Rob that you get offered products to review.

replies.author: @bobdring8857
replies.text: I use the block to dress the rotary grinding stones.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i agree mate these multi tools are great to have around for a ton of little jobs that would be super awkward without them - it astounds me too - i really didn't think my channel was that big

replies.author: @dav1dbone
replies.text: @bobdring8857 Ah ha, thanks for that, need to try that out.

replies.author: @dav1dbone
replies.text: @bobdring8857 Quite a satisfying way to clean the grinding stones(only works for alloy, generally only alloy that clogs it anyway), make up a weak solution of sodium hydroxide in an old jar and drop your grinding wheels in. Preferably outdoors or in a fume hood as it releases hydrogen. Stones are ready for use again.
